nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving coastal lagoons, swamps, canals, and tidal flats along the Yucatan Peninsula; highly vegetated shallow margins with access to brackish and marine conditions

decorPreferences: dense planting (hornwort, water sprite, vallisneria, floating plants)
open swimming lanes for male dorsal display
algae-covered surfaces and rocks for grazing
driftwood or smooth rocks as territory markers

diet.notes: The Yucatan Molly leans heavily toward plant matter in the wild, where fish feed primarily on algae and epiphytic growth (microscopic organisms and detritus that grow on plant surfaces). The staple diet in the aquarium should reflect this: algae wafers, spirulina flakes, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, spinach, and cucumber, and vegetable-based pellets are all appropriate. High-quality flake, small pellets, and occasional frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworm can be offered as protein supplements. Consistent access to algae growth or supplemental greens tends to reduce nibbling on soft-leaved aquarium plants. Feeding two to three small portions daily is generally preferred over one large feeding.

avoidWith: cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (fry predation risk)
slow-moving or long-finned fish (fin nibbling risk)
small fish that may be harassed by dominant males

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Dnoerholm; 4778x3185. Dominant male displaying its fully erect, iridescent blue-spotted sailfin dorsal in a natural aquascape, with a second individual alongside; sharp, colorful, showcases the species' hallmark feature.
Photo by Massysmith; 2611x1958. Tack-sharp full-body lateral view of a vivid orange/gold male, whole animal and raised sailfin in frame; the crispest and most saturated single-specimen shot.
Photo by Matteo Cassella; large size. Male with fully erect blue-spotted sailfin over green algae plus a second molly, showing social/shoaling behaviour and the display fin from a different angle.

summary: The Yucatan Molly (Poecilia velifera) is the largest of the sailfin mollies, with dominant males growing a sail-like dorsal fin that can match their full body depth. Endemic to Mexico’s Yucatan Coast, it thrives in hard, alkaline water and tolerates brackish conditions well.
careNotes:
The Yucatan Molly (Poecilia velifera) is widely regarded as the largest of the sailfin mollies, with males capable of reaching 15–18 cm in total length and developing a spectacular dorsal fin that can equal or exceed their body depth when fully erect. It is closely related to the Sailfin Molly ([fw-fish-092]), which overlaps in appearance and is frequently confused with it in the trade. Poecilia velifera can be distinguished by a higher dorsal fin ray count — 18 to 19 rays compared to 14 in the Sailfin Molly — as well as its larger adult size. The species is endemic to the coastal lagoons and tidal flats of the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico.

A tank of at least 200 L with a minimum 150 cm footprint is strongly recommended to give males room to display and to accommodate a healthy social group. Water should be hard and alkaline — gH 15–30, kH 8–20, pH 7.5–8.5 — as soft or acidic conditions are associated with increased disease susceptibility, fin deterioration, and shortened lifespan. Mineral supplementation is advisable in areas where tap water is naturally soft. A ratio of two to three females per male is generally recommended to distribute male harassment across the group.

The Yucatan Molly is euryhaline, meaning it naturally inhabits brackish and occasionally marine-adjacent waters. Many keepers report improved vitality, reduced fungal and bacterial infections, and better fin condition when low-level marine salt is added to raise specific gravity to around SG 1.002–1.005. This is considered beneficial but not strictly required for the species to thrive.

Numerous selectively bred colour and fin forms are available in the hobby, including Lyretail (a lyre-shaped caudal fin with extended dorsal), Marble (mottled black-and-white patterning), and Silver (platinum) morphs. Captive-bred strains are often considered hardier than wild-type fish and tend to adapt more readily to standard tap water. Fish sold as 'Giant Sailfin Molly' or 'Yucatan Molly' in the trade may sometimes be hybrids between Poecilia velifera and the Sailfin Molly; males of pure Poecilia velifera are best identified by the high dorsal ray count.
breedingNotes: The Yucatan Molly is a prolific livebearer, with gestation lasting approximately 6 to 8 weeks and broods typically ranging from 20 to 80 or more fry depending on female size and condition. Females are capable of storing sperm and can produce multiple successive broods without re-mating. Fry are born comparatively large for a livebearer and will accept finely crushed flake, spirulina powder, and micro-pellets from birth. No parental care is provided, and adults will eat fry given the opportunity; dense planting with floating plants and fine-leaved species substantially improves fry survival by offering refuge. Maintaining a ratio of two to three females per male reduces male harassment and stress on individual females. Males begin developing the characteristic dorsal sail as they approach sexual maturity, typically around 3 to 4 months of age.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Poecilia velifera
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 18 cm TL), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico), habitat, diet, breeding, and euryhaline ecology. Distinguishes P. velifera from P. latipinna by higher dorsal ray count (18–19 vs 14).

additional.name: FishBase — Poecilia velifera
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 18 cm TL), temperature tolerance (22–28°C), native range restricted to Yucatan Peninsula, taxonomy (Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes), and habitat type. Confirmed euryhaline designation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Molly Fish Care
additional.notes: General molly care guide covering all common Poecilia species. Tank size 29–55 gal (110–208 L) for larger mollies; 200 L chosen for P. velifera given its larger adult size vs P. latipinna. Confirmed temperature 24–27°C, salt tolerance, hard alkaline preference, and herbivore-leaning omnivore diet.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sailfin Molly species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed P. velifera as the largest sailfin molly species, endemic to Yucatan coastal waters, with dorsal fin reaching full body height in dominant males. Notes on selectively-bred forms (Lyretail, Marble, Silver) and trade identification challenges between P. velifera and P. latipinna hybrids.

fish.breedingNotes: The Yucatan Molly gives birth to live fry after a gestation of approximately 6 to 8 weeks, with broods of 20 to 80 or more fry per drop. Females can store sperm and produce successive broods without re-mating. No specialised breeding setup is required beyond maintaining a ratio of two to three females per male and providing dense planting for fry to hide in. Fry will accept finely crushed flake and spirulina powder from birth.
